title: Exercise7: Explorative Factor Analysis I
text:
Install and load packages
Then, we load the necessary R packages with the “pacman” package.

##load and install packages
if (!require("pacman")) {install.packages("pacman"); library(pacman)}

Lade nötiges Paket: pacman

p_load(tidyverse, car, corpcor, corrplot, psych, GPArotation)

title: Exercise7: Explorative Factor Analysis I
text:
Read data
For this exercise, we again use the data set from the European Social Survey: ESS Round 8 (2016): integrated file, edition 2.2, to be found at: https://ess-search.nsd.no/en/study/f8e11f55-0c14-4ab3-abde-96d3f14d3c76

#load data
df <- read.csv("ESS8e02_2.csv")

This time we create a filtered data set for the Austrian respondents with the following variables: - How interested in politics (polintr) - Able to take active role in political group (actrolga) - Confident in own ability to participate (cptppola) - Contacted politicians or government officials last 12 months (contplt) - Worked in political party or action group last 12 months (wrkprty) - Worked in another organization or association last 12 months (wrkorg) - Worn or displayed a campaign badge/sticker last 12 months (badge) - Signed petition last 12 months (sgnptit) - Taken part in lawful public demonstration last 12 months (pbldmn) - Boycotted certain products last 12 months (bctprd) - Posted or shared anything about politics online last 12 months (pstplonl)

#Filter cases and choose variables
df1 <- df %>% 
  filter(cntry == "AT") %>%
  dplyr::select(polintr, actrolga, cptppola, contplt, wrkprty, wrkorg, badge, sgnptit, pbldmn, bctprd, pstplonl)


#inspect datframe
glimpse(df1)
class(df1)
head(df1)
View(df1)

Next, we define the missing/invalid values as “NA”. Remember: In order to identify these values, we check the associated code book! Fortunately, for all eleven variables the missing values are coded in the same way: 7 = refusal, 8 = don’t know, 9 = no answer. Thus, we can recode all values in the data with just one line of code.
To do this, we use the mutate_all() function from the dplyr package, which is part of the Tidyverse. The “~ifelse(. %in% c(7, 8, 9), NA, .)” statement checks whether each value in the variables is either 7, 8, or 9. If so, the value is replaced with NA; otherwise, the original value is retained. Alternatively, we can proceed as in Exercise 5 and apply the “Recode” function to each variable separately. This takes a bit of work, but it works the same way.

#Recode missing values
df1 <- df1 %>%
  mutate_all(~ifelse(. %in% c(7, 8, 9), NA, .))

We check with View() if it worked.

View(df1)

For further analysis, we exclude the cases with missing values using the “drop_na” function. We apply it to the entire data set because we exclude all cases with missing values at once. With View() we can look at the filtered data set and check if it worked.

#Exclude cases with missing values
df1 <- drop_na(df1)

We will reuse the data in Exercise 8, which is why we export it into a .csv-File and an .RData file.

#Save dataframe
write.csv(df1, file = "ESS8e02_2_AT_PCA.csv")
save(df1, file = "df1.RData")

title: Exercise7: Explorative Factor Analysis I
section: Correlation matrix and check of requirements
text:
Correlation matrix and check of requirements
First, we use the “cor” function to output the correlation matrix and assign it to a new “R_df1” object. The correlation patterns are easier to inspect if we plot the matrix using the corrplot function. Factor analysis is only useful if the different variables are also correlated with each other (r > 0), but not too strongly (r < 0.9), because if two variables are perfectly correlated with each other, they are likely to measure the same construct.

#Table
R_df1 <- df1 %>%
  cor(.)%>%
  round(., 2)# rounded to two decimals
R_df1

#Graph
corrplot(R_df1)








In our case, the correlation matrix shows consistently weak to moderate correlations ( 0.1 < r < 0.5). Using the Bartlett test, we check whether the correlation matrix deviates significantly from an identity matrix in which all correlations are 0. To do this, we apply the “cortest.bartlett” function to our R matrix. Here, we also have to specify the size of the sample, i.e., the number of cases in our data set, as an argument. Alternatively, we can also apply the function directly to our data set “df1”. We can ignore the warning that R now gives us.
In addition, we calculate the Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO) measures of sampling adequacy (MSA) for the variables. They indicate whether there is enough data for each factor to obtain reliable results for factor analysis. The values should be at least > 0.5.

#Bartlett (should be significant, p<0.05)
cortest.bartlett(R_df1, n = 1868)#calculated based on the R matrix

$chisq
[1] 4342.212

$p.value
[1] 0

$df
[1] 55

cortest.bartlett(df1)#alternative: calculated directly from the data

R was not square, finding R from data


$chisq
[1] 4348.039

$p.value
[1] 0

$df
[1] 55

#KMO (Values should be at least > 0.5)
KMO(R_df1)

Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin factor adequacy
Call: KMO(r = R_df1)
Overall MSA =  0.81
MSA for each item = 
 polintr actrolga cptppola  contplt  wrkprty   wrkorg    badge  sgnptit 
    0.91     0.72     0.72     0.84     0.82     0.88     0.86     0.85 
  pbldmn   bctprd pstplonl 
    0.87     0.85     0.87 


The Bartlett test is significant, i.e. the overall correlations are significantly different from zero. The MSA values according to KMO are all greater than 0.7, i.e., in the acceptable range of values. From the results we can conclude that our data are suitable for factor analysis.

title: Exercise7: Explorative Factor Analysis I
text:
Factor extraction
We first perform a principal component analysis (PCA) without rotation to make a decision on how many factors (components) we want to extract. We use the “principal” function from the “psych” package and set the number of factors (nfactors) equal to the number of variables (=length of the data vector; length(df1)) and the rotation method (rotate) to “none”. We use the correlation matrix “R_df1” as a basis. However, you can also use the “df1” data set directly.
We also use the “plot” function to create a scree plot in which we plot the eigenvalues for the factors. With “abline(h=1)” we add a horizontal line at value 1 to better check the Kaiser criterion (eigenvalue h > 1).

  # Unrotated PCA
pc1 <- principal(R_df1, nfactors = length(df1), rotate="none")
pc1

Principal Components Analysis
Call: principal(r = R_df1, nfactors = length(df1), rotate = "none")
Standardized loadings (pattern matrix) based upon correlation matrix
           PC1   PC2   PC3   PC4   PC5   PC6   PC7   PC8   PC9  PC10  PC11 h2

cptppola -0.69  0.50  0.28  0.11  0.04  0.02  0.10  0.19  0.11  0.01 -0.36  1

Proportion Var        0.32 0.11 0.10 0.08 0.07 0.06 0.06 0.06 0.06 0.05 0.02
Cumulative Var        0.32 0.43 0.53 0.61 0.68 0.75 0.81 0.87 0.92 0.98 1.00

Cumulative Proportion 0.32 0.43 0.53 0.61 0.68 0.75 0.81 0.87 0.92 0.98 1.00

Mean item complexity =  4.2
Test of the hypothesis that 11 components are sufficient.

The root mean square of the residuals (RMSR) is  0 

Fit based upon off diagonal values = 1

# Screeplot
 plot(pc1$values, type = 'b')#Elbow diagram on the eigenvalues
 abline(h = 1)#we add a horizontal line at value 1








First we see the R output for our call. The following values are returned for each extracted component: - SS loadings: These are the eigenvalues (sum of squared factor loadings) of the factors. - Proportion Var: If you divide the eigenvalue by the number of components, you get the share of the variance explained by the factor in relation to the total variance. For example, PC1 explains 32% of the total variance. - Cumulative Var: The explained variance is summed up over all components. - Proportion Explained: sets the variance explained by the components in relation to the total explained variance, i.e. here the explained variance always adds up to 1, while Proportion Var only adds up to 1 if the entire variance in the data set is explained by the components. Because we extracted the maximum number of factors here, Proportion Var and Proportion Explained are the same. - Cumulative Proportion: The Proportion Explained is summed up over all components.
Next, we see a table (data.frame) that contains the correlations between variables and components (factor loadings) in the first columns. Since these are still based on the unrotated matrix, they are not meaningful and we do not interpret them further. This is followed by a column (h2) showing the communalities, i.e. the variance of a variable explained by all factors together. Since we extracted as many factors as variables in our first PCA, the communalities for all variables are 1. The following column contains values for the “uniqueness” (u2), i.e. the unexplained part of the variance of a variable (h2-1). This is followed by the “com” column, which lists a measure of the complexity of the variable. Complexity represents the number of components required to explain the observed variable. A perfect solution with a simple structure would have a complexity of 1 since each item would only load on one factor.
We can see from the scree plot and the table with the eigenvalues (SS loadings) that the third component still has an eigenvalue greater than 1, but all the others are below it. Therefore, for further analysis, we extract three factors.
We now repeat the PCA and enter “nfactors = 3” and the rotation method “rotate = oblimin”, i.e. we rotate in such a way that variables only load on one factor if possible and allow the factors to correlate with each other.

  # Rotated PCA

pca2 <- principal(R_df1, nfactors = 3, rotate="oblimin")
pca2

Principal Components Analysis

pstplonl -0.13  0.66 -0.10 0.48 0.52 1.1

                       TC1  TC2  TC3
SS loadings           2.09 2.00 1.75
Proportion Var        0.19 0.18 0.16
Cumulative Var        0.19 0.37 0.53
Proportion Explained  0.36 0.34 0.30

 With component correlations of 
      TC1   TC2   TC3
TC1  1.00 -0.33 -0.34
TC2 -0.33  1.00  0.30
TC3 -0.34  0.30  1.00

Mean item complexity =  1.2
Test of the hypothesis that 3 components are sufficient.

The root mean square of the residuals (RMSR) is  0.09 

Fit based upon off diagonal values = 0.88


The output is the same as the previous one, but now only three components are extracted and accordingly we only get information for these three components. In the “Proportion Var” row, we see that the first factor explains 19% of the total variance, the second factor 18%, and the third 16%. Together, all three factors explain 53% of the total variance in the variables (see the “Cumulative Var” row, which sums up the explained variance from factor to factor).
A table with the correlations between the components is now also displayed in the R Console. We see that all three factors correlate moderately with each other.
In order to be able to interpret the factors, we look at the table with the factor loadings (data.frame). We can make this even clearer with the following code so that factor loadings below 0.4 are not displayed, and factor loadings are sorted by size.

# Clear pattern matrix
print.psych(pca2, cut = 0.4, sort = TRUE)

Principal Components Analysis

Standardized loadings (pattern matrix) based upon correlation matrix

The root mean square of the residuals (RMSR) is  0.09 

Fit based upon off diagonal values = 0.88


Now we can clearly see which items load on which component. Factor 1 summarizes the items cptppola (confidence in one’s own ability to participate), actrolga (activity in a political group) and polintr (political interest). This could be interpreted as “belief in political effectiveness”. The items pstplonl (online posts), sgnptit (petitions), pbldmn (demonstrations), bctprd (boycott) load at factor 2. These all involve more or less symbolic political activities. Factor 3 also includes the items wrkprty (work for a political party) and contplt (contacts with politics), and one could describe it as more practical political activities.
Can you think of alternative labels for the three factors? What happened to the badge variable?
Note: These interpretations are always very subjective, i.e. there is not always a clear and correct solution.

title: Exercise 9: Cluster Analysis
text: Cluster analysis (or clustering) is a technique used to group data points based on their similarity. In this exercise, you will perform hierarchical clustering using Euclidean distance and Ward’s method. By the end of this tutorial, you will: - Learn how to prepare the dataset and select the respective variables, - Understand the step-by-step process of hierarchical clustering, - Learn to evaluate clustering results using dendrograms, scatterplots, elbow plots, and silhouette plots, - Understand how to report the cluster results.

title: Exercise 9: Cluster Analysis
text:
Read data
For this exercise, we will use the data (n=1027) from a representative sample of the Dutch population. The data is described in this paper:
Smit, E. S., & Bol, N. (2019). From self-reliers to expert-dependents:identifying classes based on health-related need for autonomy and need for external control among mobile users. Media Psychology, 23(3), 391–414. https://doi.org/10.1080/15213269.2019.1604235
Note: The authors perform a slightly different clustering method, namely latent class analysis. However, as you will see, our cluster analysis will produce similar results.

#load data
df <- read.csv("Dataset_for_HCOS.csv", sep = ";")


Exclude Cases with Missing Values
For further analysis, we want to exclude the cases with missing values.The ‘anyNA’ function tells us that the data set includes missing values (NA). We use ‘drop_na’ as in the previous exercises to exclude these cases.

#exclude missing cases
anyNA(df)

[1] TRUE

df <- drop_na(df)

#Inspect the dataset Cases with missing values have been excluded, so we now have n=1026 cases.

#inspect dataset

glimpse(df)
class(df)
head(df)
View(df)



Select Variables for Clustering
In this tutorial, we want to understand the individual differences in need for autonomy and need for external control regarding health-related decisions among users of mobile technologies (=person-oriented technique).
RQ1: Which subtypes can be identified among mobile users based on their need for autonomy and need for external control regarding health and health-related decisions?
We will use the following variables for our clustering: - HCOS_autonomy: Preference for autonomy (e.g., “How likely are you to: Set your own goals?”) - HCOS_control_expert: Preference for expert control(e.g., “How likely are you to: Have an expert set goals for you?”) - HCOS_control_peer: Preference for peer control (e.g., “How likely are you to: Ask friends or family to help you set goals?”)

# Select only the variables of interest
df1 <- df[, c("HCOS_autonomy", "HCOS_control_expert", "HCOS_control_peer")]

# Display the first few rows of the selected data

title: Exercise 9: Cluster Analysis
text:
Perform Hierarchical Clustering
We’ll apply Ward’s method to create clusters and plot the dendrogram.

# Perform hierarchical clustering
hc1 <- hclust(distance_matrix, method = "ward.D2")

# Display the dendrogram
plot(hc1, 
     main = "Dendrogram for Hierarchical Clustering", 
     xlab = "Observations", 
     ylab = "Height")








Interpretation of the plot: The dendrogram represents how the clusters are formed step-by-step by merging individual data points (or smaller clusters) into larger clusters. The dendrogram is like a tree, with each branch representing a data point or a cluster of data points. The further down the tree you go, the more similar the data points are to each other. The height at which two branches (clusters) join represents the dissimilarity (or distance) between them. A lower height means the clusters are very similar, while a higher height means the clusters are more different. By drawing a horizontal line across the tree at a certain height (cutting through the branches), you can decide how many clusters you want.

title: Exercise 9: Cluster Analysis
text:
Determine Optimal Number of Cluster
We use two visualization methods to determine the optimal number of clusters, namely the elbow criterion and silhouette analysis.

#Elbow method
fviz_nbclust(cluster_df1, FUN = hcut, method = "wss") + labs(subtitle = "Elbow method")

Warning: The `size` argument of `element_line()` is deprecated as of ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use the `linewidth` argument instead.
ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the ggpubr package.
  Please report the issue at <https://github.com/kassambara/ggpubr/issues>.


Warning: The `size` argument of `element_rect()` is deprecated as of ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use the `linewidth` argument instead.
ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the ggpubr package.
  Please report the issue at <https://github.com/kassambara/ggpubr/issues>.


Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.
ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the ggpubr package.
  Please report the issue at <https://github.com/kassambara/ggpubr/issues>.








## Silhouette method
fviz_nbclust(cluster_df1, FUN = hcut, method = "silhouette")+labs(subtitle = "Silhouette method")








Interpretation of plots:
The Elbow Method visualizes the total within-cluster sum of squares (WSS) as a function of the number of clusters. The goal is to identify the “elbow point,” where the rate of decrease in WSS slows down. This suggests a good balance between minimizing WSS and avoiding too many clusters.Examine the plot and look for the point where the curve forms an “elbow” or levels off. This point represents the optimal number of clusters. If no clear elbow exists, use the Silhouette Method as a supplementary approach.
The Silhouette Method evaluates how well-separated the clusters are by calculating the silhouette width, a measure of how similar an object is to its own cluster compared to others. The higher the average silhouette width, the better-defined the clusters. Look for the number of clusters with the highest average silhouette width in the plot. In our case three clusters.

title: Exercise 9: Cluster Analysis
section: Cut the Dendrogram to Form Clusters and Visualize the Clusters
text:
Cut the Dendrogram to Form Clusters and Visualize the Clusters
Decide on the number of clusters and assign cluster labels to the data (e.g., 3).

# Cut the dendrogram/tree to create 3 clusters
clusters <- cutree(hc1, k = 3)

# Add cluster membership to the original dataset
df1$Cluster <- clusters

#Visualize the clusters in the dendrogram
plot(hc1, cex = 0.6)
rect.hclust(hc1, k = 3, border = 2:5)







# Scatterplot of the first two dimensions (scaled data)
plot(cluster_df1[, 1:2], 
     col = clusters, 
     pch = 19, 
     main = "Scatterplot of Clusters",
     xlab = "First Dimension (scaled)",
     ylab = "Second Dimension (scaled)")







# Cluster plot
fviz_cluster(list(data = cluster_df1, cluster = clusters))








Interpretation of plots:
The dendrogram provides insight into the relationship between the data points and how they are grouped together based on their similarities. The rectangular borders highlight the three main clusters, showing the level at which each cluster was formed.
The scatterplot visualizes the first two principal components (or dimensions) of the scaled data, where each point represents an observation, colored according to its assigned cluster. Attention: The dimensions for the scatter plot or cluster plot are not necessarily the first two variables entered into the clustering. Instead, they typically represent the first two principal components. Basically, R performs a factor analysis to visualize the three-dimensional data.
The cluster plot is a more refined cluster plot that displays the data points in a 2D plot, with the clusters clearly marked by different colors and possibly showing the cluster centers.

# Calculate the mean of each variable for each cluster
cluster_summary <- aggregate(df1, by = list(Cluster = clusters), FUN = mean)
cluster_size <- table(clusters)

# Add the size of each cluster to the cluster_summary
cluster_summary$Members <- cluster_size[as.factor(cluster_summary$Cluster)]

# Calculate and add the percentage of members per cluster
total_members <- sum(cluster_size)
cluster_summary$Percentage <- (cluster_summary$Members / total_members) * 100

# Display cluster summary
cluster_summary

  Cluster HCOS_autonomy HCOS_control_expert HCOS_control_peer Cluster Members
1       1      5.960291            3.762864          4.354027       1     447
2       2      6.243848            2.894295          2.045861       2     447
3       3      4.253788            4.030303          3.035985       3     132
  Percentage
1   43.56725
2   43.56725
3   12.86550


Description:
Cluster 1: Self-reliers The table shows that respondents (n= 461) in cluster 1 score highly on autonomy orientatation with 6.16 (on a 7-point-scale), while their scores for control orientation were – compared with the other clusters – less than average (control orientation – professionals: M = 4.60; control orientation – peers: M = 2.60). This cluster represents the largest group in our study population (44%).
Cluster 2: Confirmation seekers The second cluster of respondents was labelled as confirmation seekers (n = 396). This cluster of respondents reported relatively high scores for both autonomous (M = 6.13) and control orientation (control orientation – professionals: M = 4.60; control orientation – peers: M = 3.60).
Cluster 3: Expert dependents The third cluster of respondents was labelled as expert-dependents (n = 169). Respondents reported a less than average score for autonomy orientation (M = 4.43), both scores for control orientation were above average relative to the total sample (control orientation – professionals: M = 3.60; control orientation – peers: M = 3.78). The expert-dependents encompassed the smallest group in our sample (16%).
